meta.description: A scientific validation platform for wellness practitioners — early validation with weak evidence and bias flags

project.name: GST Body
project.description: A scientific validation platform for wellness practitioners to design, test, and launch evidence-based body composition programs.
project.primaryGoal: Launch certification program with 10+ paying clinics in the first quarter

mvvStatement.mission: Make body composition science accessible to every wellness practitioner
mvvStatement.vision: A world where every fitness program is built on validated physiology, not trends
founderAssumptions: Clinics will pay for validated protocols
unresolvedTensions: Depth vs accessibility of science content

project.conceptIntake: GST Body provides validated body composition protocols for wellness clinics. Most fitness programs are built on trends and bro-science rather than actual physiology research. GST Body bridges this gap by providing clinics with evidence-based protocols, assessment tools, and certification programs that differentiate them from unqualified trainers who undercut on price.

personas.name: Wellness Clinic Owner
personas.primaryPain: Competing with unqualified trainers who undercut on price — needs a science-backed differentiator
personas.context: Owns a clinic with 3-10 staff, sees clients 30+ sessions/week, wants to justify premium pricing

personas.name: Certified Practitioner
personas.primaryPain: Hit a ceiling with current certifications and wants to offer advanced body composition services without going back to school
personas.context: Has personal training cert, 3-5 years experience, wants to specialize

offers.name: GST Body Certification Program
offers.valueProposition: Turn your clinic into an evidence-based body composition authority with certified protocols, assessment tools, and marketing support

hypotheses.title: Clinics need science-backed programs to compete
hypotheses.statement: Wellness clinic owners believe that offering evidence-based protocols differentiates them from unqualified trainers and justifies premium pricing of $150+/session.

riskRank.rationale: Core value prop — if clinics don't value science differentiation, the premise fails

hypotheses.title: Practitioners can learn composition science
hypotheses.statement: Wellness practitioners without physiology backgrounds can understand and apply validated body composition protocols after a 40-hour training program.

riskRank.rationale: Delivery depends on practitioners actually applying the protocols

hypotheses.title: Clinics will pay $3k for certification
hypotheses.statement: Wellness clinic owners will invest $2,000-$4,000 in a certification program that differentiates their practice and enables premium pricing.

riskRank.rationale: Revenue model depends on this price point being acceptable

hypotheses.title: Program completion improves client outcomes
hypotheses.statement: Clinics running GST Body programs see measurable improvements in client body composition metrics within 12 weeks compared to standard programming.

riskRank.rationale: Long-term value and retention depend on proven results

hypotheses.title: Premium pricing is sustainable long-term
hypotheses.statement: Clinics can maintain premium pricing ($150+/session) using evidence-based differentiation without losing clients to cheaper alternatives over 12+ months.

riskRank.rationale: Unit economics depend on sustained pricing power

evidenceItems.summary: 3 of 5 clinic owners said science differentiation 'sounds interesting' in initial conversations — weak interest signal
evidenceItems.rawText: I asked 5 clinic owners if they'd use science-based protocols. 3 said 'that sounds interesting' but none committed. One said 'I'd need to see the data first.' Another asked about pricing but didn't follow up.

biasFlags.explanation: Only 3 of 5 said it 'sounds interesting' — this is polite praise, not purchase intent
'Sounds interesting' is non-committal language indicating low commitment

qualityReview.recommendedDisconfirmationTest: Run a landing page test with a pre-order button to measure true willingness to pay over stated interest

evidenceItems.summary: One clinic owner 'loves the idea' of certification but didn't act on it
evidenceItems.rawText: A clinic owner said they 'love the idea' of having a certification. They asked about pricing and timeline but didn't follow up when given details.

biasFlags.explanation: 'Love the idea' is a classic polite praise signal — enthusiasm for the concept, not a purchase
Founder is interpreting expressed interest as validation rather than capturing actual behavior

qualityReview.recommendedDisconfirmationTest: Create a fake-door test: see if clinics actually enter payment info for a deposit on the certification

pmfAssessment.explanation: Early validation stage. 2 evidence items collected, both flagged for significant bias. Evidence distortion coefficient is 0.42 — biased evidence is suppressing confidence. Need to run disconfirmation tests before proceeding.
